description:
Islamic cosmology describes how Allah created the heavens and earth through His command "Kun!" (Be!).
                In six distinct periods or "days," all of existence came into being from absolute nothingness,
                culminating in the creation of humanity as khalifa (steward) of the Earth.
name: Islamic Cosmology

keyMyths.title: The Creation from Nothingness
keyMyths.description: Allah created the universe ex nihilo, declaring "Kun!" (Be!), instantly bringing existence into being. This highlights the absolute power and authority of Allah as the sole creator.

keyMyths.title: The Six Days of Creation
keyMyths.description: The creation of the heavens and the earth unfolded over six distinct periods, often interpreted as epochs rather than literal 24-hour days. These stages involved the formation of the heavens, the Earth, mountains, seas, and all living creatures.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Adam
keyMyths.description: Adam, the first human, was created from clay and given life by Allah. He was granted knowledge and dominion over the Earth, establishing humanity's role as Allah's stewards (khalifa).

keyMyths.title: The Angels' Questioning
keyMyths.description: Before creating Adam, Allah informed the angels of His intention to create a human being as His representative on Earth. The angels questioned this decision, expressing concern about potential corruption, but Allah assured them of His superior knowledge.

keyMyths.title: The Throne of Allah (Arsh) and the Footstool (Kursi)
keyMyths.description: Allah's throne (Arsh) is described as the highest point of creation, symbolizing His sovereignty and power over all existence. The Footstool (Kursi) is often interpreted as a symbol of Allah's knowledge encompassing the heavens and the Earth.

sources.name: The Quran
sources.description: The central religious text of Islam, believed by Muslims to be the word of God as revealed to Muhammad.
sources.title: The Quran

sources.name: Sahih al-Bukhari
sources.description: A collection of hadith (sayings and actions of Muhammad) considered to be the most authentic.
sources.title: Sahih al-Bukhari

sources.name: Ibn Kathir's Tafsir
sources.description: A classic and widely respected commentary on the Quran by the 14th-century scholar Ibn Kathir.
sources.title: Ibn Kathir's Tafsir

sources.name: Al-Ghazali, The Alchemy of Happiness
sources.description: A key text by the influential theologian and philosopher Al-Ghazali, which touches upon cosmological concepts.
sources.title: Al-Ghazali, The Alchemy of Happiness

sources.name: Seyyed Hossein Nasr, An Introduction to Islamic Cosmological Doctrines
sources.description: A scholarly work that provides a comprehensive overview of Islamic cosmological traditions.
sources.title: Seyyed Hossein Nasr, An Introduction to Islamic Cosmological Doctrines

worshipPractices.name: Daily Prayers (Salat)
worshipPractices.description: Muslims perform five daily prayers facing the Kaaba in Mecca. These prayers serve as a direct connection to Allah and a reminder of His presence and guidance throughout the day. The prayers are structured with specific recitations and movements that reflect submission and devotion.

worshipPractices.name: Ramadan Fasting (Sawm)
worshipPractices.description: During the month of Ramadan, Muslims abstain from food and drink from dawn until sunset. This act of fasting is a spiritual discipline aimed at purification, empathy for the less fortunate, and increased devotion to Allah. It culminates in the celebration of Eid al-Fitr.

festivals.name: Eid al-Fitr
festivals.description: A joyous celebration marking the end of the month-long fast of Ramadan. Families gather for special meals, give gifts, and offer prayers of gratitude to Allah. It is a time for forgiveness, reconciliation, and strengthening community bonds.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha
festivals.description: Commemorates Prophet Ibrahim's (Abraham) willingness to sacrifice his son as an act of obedience to Allah. Muslims sacrifice an animal (usually a goat or sheep) and share the meat with family, friends, and the poor. It emphasizes sacrifice, devotion, and compassion.

cultural.modernLegacy: Islamic cosmology continues to influence modern architectural designs, particularly in mosques and other Islamic buildings, which often incorporate symbolic representations of the heavens and the universe. Furthermore, it inspires contemporary Islamic art and literature, prompting reflections on humanity's place within creation and the relationship with the divine.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Similar to the concept of the cosmic mountain Qaf, Yggdrasil serves as a central axis connecting different realms of existence in Norse cosmology. Both represent a structured universe with interconnected layers.

The concept of cyclical creation and destruction in Hinduism, with Brahma's day representing a period of creation and his night a period of dissolution, parallels the Islamic emphasis on Allah's continuous creation and control over existence, although Islamic cosmology is not cyclical.

The initial state of 'nothingness' before creation in Islamic cosmology mirrors the primordial void of Chaos in Greek mythology, from which the first gods and the universe emerged. Both represent the unformed state before divine intervention shaped reality.

Atum, the creator god in Egyptian mythology, created the world from Nun, the primordial waters of chaos, similar to Allah's creation from nothingness (ex nihilo). Both Atum and Allah are considered the first cause and origin of all things.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Represents the seven heavens described in the Quran, each made of a different material and inhabited by different beings.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: Serve as lamps in the lowest heaven and are considered signs of Allah's power and guidance.

associations.name: The Throne (Arsh)
associations.significance: Represents Allah's ultimate authority and is the highest point in creation, symbolizing His dominion over all things.

associations.name: Al-Kursi (The Footstool)
associations.significance: Symbolizes Allah's power and knowledge encompassing the heavens and the earth, representing a lower level of dominion than the Throne.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Is the source of all life and a fundamental element from which Allah created the universe, representing purity and creation.

associations.name: Balance (Mizan)
associations.significance: Represents the perfect order and harmony that Allah established in the cosmos, and the accountability on the Day of Judgement.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Is a source of light and warmth created by Allah to sustain life on Earth, marking time and seasons as a sign of His providence.

companions.name: Israfil
companions.description: Israfil is the angel responsible for signaling the Day of Judgment by blowing the Trumpet, announcing the end of the current cosmic order and the beginning of the afterlife.

companions.name: Arsh
companions.description: The Arsh (Throne of God) is considered the highest point in the cosmos and symbolizes Allah's authority and dominion over all creation, including the structure of the universe.

companions.name: Al-Kursi
companions.description: Al-Kursi (Footstool of God) is a part of the Arsh and represents Allah's power and knowledge extending throughout the heavens and the earth, encompassing all of existence within Islamic cosmology.

companions.name: Jinn
companions.description: Jinn are created from smokeless fire, they exist in a realm parallel to humans. They are part of the cosmic order and can be either believers or disbelievers, interacting with the human world and influencing events.

companions.name: Angels
companions.description: Angels are beings of light created by Allah to serve Him and carry out His commands throughout the cosmos, maintaining the order and functioning of the universe according to divine will.

extendedContent.title: The Foundation: Creation from Nothingness (Ex Nihilo)
extendedContent.content:
Islamic cosmology posits a creation ex nihilo, meaning 'out of nothing.' This concept is fundamentally rooted in the Quran, particularly in verses like "He is the Originator of the heavens and the earth" (Quran 6:101). The act of creation is attributed solely to Allah's divine will and power, manifested through the command 'Kun!' (Be!). This instantaneous command brought the entire universe into existence from a state of absolute non-existence. The Quranic verses describing the creation emphasize Allah's complete sovereignty and independence in the act of bringing the cosmos into being. This initial creation laid the foundation for the subsequent stages of cosmic development.

Further elaboration on the creation process is found in various Islamic traditions and commentaries. Some interpretations suggest that before the tangible universe existed, there was only Allah and His 'Arsh (Throne) resting upon water. The creation then proceeded in six 'days' or periods, each representing a significant stage in the formation of the heavens, earth, and all that lies between. These 'days' are not necessarily literal 24-hour periods, but rather epochs of divine activity. The concept of ex nihilo is crucial in understanding the Islamic worldview, as it underscores the absolute dependence of the entire creation on Allah.

This doctrine has profound theological implications. It establishes a clear distinction between the Creator and the creation, emphasizing Allah's transcendence and uniqueness. It also highlights the contingency of the universe, meaning that it is entirely dependent on Allah for its existence and sustenance. This understanding fosters a sense of humility and gratitude in believers, recognizing their reliance on the divine for every aspect of their lives and the world around them.

extendedContent.title: The Six Days of Creation: A Gradual Unfolding
extendedContent.content:
The Quran outlines the creation of the universe in six 'days' (Ayyam), as stated in verses such as "Indeed, your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days" (Quran 7:54). While the exact nature and duration of these 'days' are subject to interpretation, they are generally understood as distinct phases or periods in the formation of the cosmos. The initial stages likely involved the separation of the heavens and the earth from a single, primordial mass. This is hinted at in the Quranic verse: "Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, then We separated them…?" (Quran 21:30).

The subsequent stages involved the establishment of the heavens, including the celestial bodies and the arrangement of the stars. The earth was then prepared for life, with the creation of mountains, rivers, and vegetation. The final stages culminated in the creation of animals and, ultimately, humanity. Islamic tradition often elaborates on the details of these stages, providing accounts of the angels' involvement in the creation process and the specific order in which different creatures were brought into being. The creation of Adam, the first human, holds a special significance as he was created as Allah's khalifa (vicegerent) on Earth.

These six days are not merely a chronological account but also a testament to Allah's wisdom and power. The gradual unfolding of creation demonstrates a deliberate and purposeful design, reflecting the divine attribute of hikmah (wisdom). The sequential development also highlights the interconnectedness of all creation, emphasizing the delicate balance and harmony that exist within the universe. Understanding the six days of creation provides a framework for appreciating the complexity and beauty of the cosmos, reinforcing the belief in Allah as the ultimate Creator and Sustainer.

extendedContent.title: The Seven Heavens and the Throne (Arsh): Cosmic Structure
extendedContent.content:
Islamic cosmology describes a layered structure of the universe, typically consisting of seven heavens (samaawaat). These heavens are not merely physical layers but also represent spiritual realms or dimensions. The Quran mentions the seven heavens in various verses, such as "He is the One Who created seven heavens one above another" (Quran 67:3). These heavens are often described as being made of different materials, with the highest heaven being closest to Allah's presence. Each heaven is associated with different prophets or celestial beings, and they are believed to be populated by angels.

Above the seven heavens lies the 'Arsh (Throne) of Allah, the highest point in the cosmos and the ultimate symbol of divine authority. The 'Arsh is often described as being immense and majestic, representing Allah's absolute power and dominion over all creation. It is said to be supported by angels who constantly glorify Allah. The concept of the 'Arsh is central to Islamic theology, emphasizing Allah's transcendence and His ultimate control over the universe. The Kursi (Footstool) is also mentioned in the Quran (2:255), being a place lower than the 'Arsh and higher than all creation.

Below the earth, some traditions also describe seven earths, forming a mirror image of the heavens. These lower earths are often associated with different types of beings or realms. The overall structure of the seven heavens and seven earths emphasizes the vastness and complexity of the universe, while also highlighting the central role of Allah's 'Arsh as the ultimate source of power and authority. This cosmic structure serves as a reminder of Allah's greatness and the limitations of human understanding, encouraging humility and reverence in the face of the divine.

extendedContent.title: Angels and Jinn: Inhabitants of the Unseen Realm
extendedContent.content:
Islamic cosmology recognizes the existence of beings beyond the visible world, most notably angels and jinn. Angels are created from light and are entirely obedient to Allah, fulfilling various roles in the cosmos. They are responsible for carrying out Allah's commands, recording human actions, and glorifying Him. The archangels, such as Jibril (Gabriel), Mika'il (Michael), Israfil, and Azrael, hold prominent positions and are associated with specific tasks. Jibril is known as the messenger angel, conveying Allah's revelations to the prophets. Mika'il is associated with sustenance and rain, while Israfil will blow the trumpet to signal the Day of Judgment. Azrael is the angel of death.

Jinn, on the other hand, are created from smokeless fire and possess free will, allowing them to choose between good and evil. Some jinn are believers in Allah, while others are disbelievers. The disbelieving jinn, often referred to as shayatin (devils), are led by Iblis (Satan), who refused to prostrate before Adam and was cast out of paradise. Iblis and his followers constantly strive to mislead humanity and tempt them away from the path of righteousness. The Quran and Hadith provide numerous accounts of interactions between humans and jinn, highlighting the potential for both positive and negative influences.

The existence of angels and jinn underscores the reality of the unseen realm and the limitations of human perception. It also emphasizes the spiritual dimension of the universe and the ongoing struggle between good and evil. Believing in these unseen beings is an essential aspect of Islamic faith, fostering a sense of awareness of the spiritual forces that shape human lives and the world around them.

extendedContent.title: Eschatology: The End Times and the Day of Judgment
extendedContent.content:
Islamic cosmology includes a detailed eschatology, or doctrine of the end times. This encompasses the events leading up to the Day of Judgment (Yawm al-Qiyāmah), the resurrection of the dead, and the final reckoning before Allah. The Quran and Hadith describe a series of signs that will precede the end of the world, including widespread corruption, moral decay, and natural disasters. The appearance of the Dajjal (Antichrist), a figure of immense power and deception, is a major sign of the approaching end. The return of Prophet Isa (Jesus) to defeat the Dajjal is also a central belief in Islamic eschatology.

On the Day of Judgment, all of humanity will be resurrected and judged by Allah based on their deeds in this life. Each person's actions will be weighed on a scale, and those whose good deeds outweigh their bad deeds will be rewarded with paradise (Jannah), while those whose bad deeds outweigh their good deeds will be punished in hellfire (Jahannam). Paradise is described as a place of eternal bliss and beauty, while hellfire is a place of torment and suffering. The Quran provides vivid descriptions of both paradise and hellfire, emphasizing the ultimate consequences of human choices.

The belief in the Day of Judgment serves as a powerful motivator for righteous behavior and a deterrent against sin. It underscores the importance of accountability and the ultimate justice of Allah. Islamic eschatology emphasizes the cyclical nature of time, with the end of this world marking the beginning of the eternal afterlife. It also reinforces the belief in Allah's ultimate sovereignty and His power to bring about the final resolution of all things.
